content.plain_english: This week in Year 6 maths, your child is working on Place value. The learning focuses on: read, write, order and compare numbers up to 10,000,000; determine the value of each digit; and identify the value of each digit to three decimal places. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: In lessons, children use base ten blocks, place value charts, and number lines to explore how numbers are built from hundreds, tens, and ones. They represent the same number in different ways before working abstractly. Understanding place value is the foundation for all other maths, so lessons move carefully and thoroughly.
content.home_activity: Take a pack of playing cards (remove picture cards). Draw three or four cards and make as many different numbers as you can with those digits. Which is the biggest? The smallest? Try rounding each number to the nearest 100. No special equipment — just cards and conversation.
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to read, write, order and compare numbers up to 10,000,000?
Was it easy or tricky to determine the value of each digit? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: One of the symbols 0–9 used to write a number.
The value of a digit based on its position — units, tens, hundreds, and so on.
Split a number into its parts — e.g. 347 = 300 + 40 + 7.
Give an approximate value to the nearest 10, 100, etc.
Make a sensible guess at an answer before you calculate.

content.times_table_tip: Year 6 children use all their times tables to tackle complex problems including long multiplication, division, and fractions. Challenge them: 'What is 7 × 12?' or 'How many 8s in 72?' Rapid recall frees up thinking space for harder work.
